name: Long or Short, Heavy or Light — First Measuring
whatIsIt: Before rulers and scales, we measure by COMPARING. Longer or shorter? Taller or shorter? Heavier or lighter? We can also measure with things we have — how many hand-spans long is the table, or how many steps across the room. These are our first, hands-on ways to measure.
whyNeeded: You compare sizes every day — which pencil is longer, which bag is heavier, who is taller. Comparing and measuring with hand-spans or steps builds the idea of a UNIT, which later becomes centimetres, grams and litres.
realLifeUses: Which is longer — the pencil or the crayon?
Which school bag is heavier when packed?
Seeing who is taller when standing back to back

vocabulary.meaning: The 'one' you measure with — like one hand-span or one step.

story.title: The Table Measuring Match
story.text: Kabir wants to know how long the study table is, but there is no ruler. 'Use your hand!' says Didi. Kabir places his hand-span again and again along the edge and counts. He gets 6 hand-spans. Didi measures the same table with her bigger hand and gets 5. 'Same table, different count — because our hands are different units,' laughs Didi.
story.extractedProblem: Kabir's hand-span fits 6 times along the table. How many of Kabir's hand-spans long is the table?

visual.caption: The pencil is longer than the crayon. Compare lengths side by side, ends lined up.

teachingGallery.title: Compare and measure
teachingGallery.note: Line up ends to compare length. Count units to measure.

examples.caption: The elephant is heavier than the mouse.
Always start both objects from the same line.

standardMethod.summary: To compare, line up the ends and look. To measure, pick one unit and count how many times it fits.
steps: To compare length: put both objects side by side with one end lined up. The one that sticks out is longer.
To measure: choose a unit (a hand-span, a step, a paper clip).
Lay the unit end to end and COUNT how many fit. That count is the measure.

workedExamples.problem: A ribbon is 8 paper-clips long. A pencil is 5 paper-clips long. Which is longer, and by how many clips?
steps: 8 is more, so the ribbon is longer.

workedExamples.problem: Meena's step fits 10 times across the room; Papa's bigger step fits 7 times. Why do they get different numbers for the same room?
steps: But a step is the unit, and Papa's step is bigger.
Bigger unit → fewer of them fit. Same room, different unit.

commonMistakes.mistake: Comparing two lengths without lining up their starting ends.
commonMistakes.fix: Always start both objects at the same line. Otherwise a shorter thing can look longer.

commonMistakes.mistake: Leaving gaps or overlaps when laying units to measure.
commonMistakes.fix: Lay units end to end with NO gaps and NO overlaps, or the count will be wrong.

easy.q: An elephant and a mouse — which is heavier?
A stick is 4 paper-clips long. How many paper-clips is that?

medium.q: A rope is 9 hand-spans long and a scarf is 6 hand-spans long. How many hand-spans LONGER is the rope?
To measure a table with paper-clips, you should…

options.label: Lay clips end to end with no gaps

medium.answer: Lay clips end to end with no gaps
hintLadder: Units must touch, end to end.

challenge.q: Two ribbons look the same, but one starts further ahead. To compare fairly you must…

options.label: Line up their starting ends first
Just look at where they finish

challenge.answer: Line up their starting ends first
hintLadder: A fair race starts on the same line.

challenge.q: The room is 12 of Meena's steps across. She has walked 7 steps. How many steps are left?

questions.q: A pencil and a crayon side by side, ends lined up — the pencil sticks out. The pencil is…
A table is 7 hand-spans long. How many hand-spans is that?
A blue rope is 10 clips, a red rope is 4 clips. How many clips longer is the blue rope?
Papa's step is bigger than Meena's. For the SAME room, Papa's count will be…

options.label: Smaller — a bigger unit fits fewer times

questions.answer: Smaller — a bigger unit fits fewer times
hintLadder: Fewer big steps cross the room.

teachBackPrompt: Measure your bed (or table) in hand-spans, then ask a grown-up to measure the same thing with their hand. Explain why your two numbers are different even though it is the same bed.

revisionCard.summary: Compare by lining up ends: what sticks out is longer/taller. Measure by laying a UNIT (hand-span, step, clip) end to end with no gaps and counting. Bigger unit → fewer fit.
